turns.content: I find myself in need of some help with planning. My wife's birthday is approaching and I want to do something that goes beyond the usual dinner reservation.
That's a thoughtful impulse. When is her birthday, and do you have a general sense of what she would find meaningful — experiences, travel, something more personal?

July the 8th. She will be turning forty-five, which feels like a milestone worth honoring properly. And I think what would mean the most to her is travel. She has mentioned Cape Coast several times in the past year, always in that wistful way that tells me it is not an idle wish.

Cape Coast is a beautiful and deeply meaningful destination, especially for someone with Ghanaian heritage. The castle, the fishing villages, the coastline — it carries both historical weight and personal connection. A trip there for a milestone birthday would be significant on many levels. How realistic is it logistically? July is only about two months away, and an international trip takes planning.

That is exactly the issue. I have not begun any of the planning. Between my surgical schedule and the various things occupying my attention at work, I have let the weeks slip by. Two months is not much time to organize a trip to Ghana. There are flights to book, accommodations to arrange, and we would both need to coordinate time off. Abena is meticulous about her schedule at the pharmacy. She would need significant advance notice.

Two months is tight for Ghana but not impossible, especially if you start now. Flights to Accra from Minneapolis usually connect through JFK or Dulles, and July is a popular travel period so availability could thin out quickly. For Cape Coast specifically, you'd want to book accommodations near the coast — there are some lovely guesthouses and boutique hotels. The bigger question might be the time off. How much time would you want to spend there?

I was thinking ten days. Enough to spend meaningful time in Cape Coast without rushing, and perhaps a few days in Accra as well to visit family. We have relatives there whom Abena has not seen in several years. Combining the birthday with a family visit would make the trip feel complete rather than rushed.

Ten days is a good length. You could split it roughly into three or four days in Cape Coast for the birthday celebration and sightseeing, then shift to Accra for the family visits. That gives both parts of the trip room to breathe. For the birthday itself, are you thinking of something specific — a dinner, an excursion, something she wouldn't expect?

I had thought about arranging a private dinner near the coast. Something outdoors, with the ocean. Abena is not one for grand spectacle. She would prefer something intimate and considered. Perhaps a meal prepared with local ingredients, the two of us, the sound of the water. She carries so much responsibility in her daily life. I want to give her an evening where she carries nothing.

"An evening where she carries nothing" — that's a beautiful way to frame it. A private seaside dinner in Cape Coast is absolutely doable. Some of the guesthouses along the coast can arrange private dining with local chefs who prepare traditional dishes with fresh seafood. You might also look into places near Anomabo or Elmina, which are quieter stretches of coastline but still close to the main Cape Coast attractions. Would this be a surprise, or would you tell her about the trip in advance?

It cannot be a complete surprise because of the logistics. She would need to request leave and she is not the sort of person who appreciates having plans made entirely without her input. But I could present it as a proposal rather than a fait accompli. Something like, I have been thinking about Cape Coast for your birthday. Would you like that? And then let her help shape the details. She would enjoy the planning as much as the trip itself.

That's a wise approach. It respects her agency while still showing that you initiated the idea with care. And you're right — for someone who is precise and detail-oriented, being involved in the planning is part of the gift, not a dilution of it. When would you tell her? The sooner you start this conversation, the more time she has to arrange her pharmacy schedule.

This week, I think. I have been procrastinating not because of uncertainty about what to do, but because once I say it aloud it becomes real, and then the planning begins in earnest. But she deserves this. Forty-five years. She has built a life in a country that was not her own, raised us both up when I was drowning in residency, kept our household together with grace. A trip home to the coast is the least I can offer.

That's a wonderful tribute to her, and I think she'll hear all of that in the gesture even if you don't say it in those exact words. Tell her this week, start the planning together, and the two months will be enough. Would it help to put together a rough checklist of what needs to happen — flights, accommodations, leave requests, family coordination — so you have something concrete when you bring it up?

Yes, that would be helpful. Let me write it down. Flights, accommodations, leave for both of us, notify the family in Accra, research the private dining options near Cape Coast, and renew my passport which I believe may be expiring. Ei, there is more to do than I thought. But it will be worth it. By God's grace, we will make it happen. Thank you for helping me organize my thoughts.

Check the passport first — that one has the longest lead time and could be a bottleneck if it needs renewal. Everything else can move in parallel once that's confirmed. I hope the conversation with Abena goes beautifully this week. Happy planning.
